title: Cagliari Calcio

infobox.value: *Gli Isolani* (The Islanders) *Casteddu* (Cagliari in [Sardinian language](/wiki/Sardinian_language "Sardinian language")) *Rossoblù* (Red and Blue) *I Sardi* (The Sardinians)
30 May 1920; 105 years ago (1920-05-30) as, *Cagliari Football Club* 1935; 91 years ago (1935) as, *Unione Sportiva Cagliari*

(Italian: [[ˈkaʎʎari]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Cagliari.ogg "File:It-Cagliari.ogg")), is a professional [Italian football](/wiki/Football_in_Italy "Football in Italy") club based in [Cagliari](/wiki/Cagliari "Cagliari"), [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia"), that plays in [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A"), the first tier of [Italian football](/wiki/Italian_football "Italian football"). The club currently plays home matches at the 16,416-seat [Unipol Domus](/wiki/Unipol_Domus "Unipol Domus").

**Cagliari Calcio**, commonly referred to as **Cagliari** (Italian: [[ˈkaʎʎari]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Cagliari.ogg "File:It-Cagliari.ogg")), is a professional [Italian football](/wiki/Football_in_Italy "Football in Italy") club based in [Cagliari](/wiki/Cagliari "Cagliari"), [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia"), that plays in [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A"), the first tier of [Italian football](/wiki/Italian_football "Italian football"). The club currently plays home matches at the 16,416-seat [Unipol Domus](/wiki/Unipol_Domus "Unipol Domus").

Founded in 1920, they won their first and only *[Scudetto](/wiki/Scudetto "Scudetto")* in [1969–70](/wiki/1969%E2%80%9370_Serie_A "1969–70 Serie A"), when they were led by the [Italian national team](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team")'s all-time leading scorer, [Gigi Riva](/wiki/Gigi_Riva "Gigi Riva"). The triumph was also the first by a club from south of [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"). The club's best European performance was in the [1993–94 UEFA Cup](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_UEFA_Cup "1993–94 UEFA Cup"), reaching the semi-finals before losing to eventual winners [Internazionale](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan").

As with the flag of its city, Cagliari's main colours are blue and red. The club badge incorporates the [flag of Sardinia](/wiki/Flag_of_Sardinia "Flag of Sardinia").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cagliari_Calcio&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Before Serie A

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cagliari_Calcio&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Before Serie A")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/1930%E2%80%9331_Club_Sportivo_Cagliari.jpg/250px-1930%E2%80%9331_Club_Sportivo_Cagliari.jpg)](/wiki/File:1930%E2%80%9331_Club_Sportivo_Cagliari.jpg)

1930–31 Club Sportivo Cagliari

Cagliari became the first ever out-right champions of [Serie C](/wiki/Serie_C "Serie C") during the 1951–52 season; prior to that in the league, the championship was shared amongst more than one team. They spent the 1950s from then on in [Serie B](/wiki/Serie_B "Serie B"), losing a promotion play-off in 1954. After descending to Serie C in the early 1960s, Cagliari's rise would be meteoric, eventually achieving promotion to [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") in 1964.

### First Serie A adventure: 1964–1976

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cagliari_Calcio&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: First Serie A adventure: 1964–1976")]

The squad for the *Rossoblu'*s debut season in Serie A featured players like defender [Mario Martiradonna](/wiki/Mario_Martiradonna "Mario Martiradonna"), midfielders [Pierluigi Cera](/wiki/Pierluigi_Cera "Pierluigi Cera"), [Nené](/wiki/Claudio_Olinto_de_Carvalho "Claudio Olinto de Carvalho") and [Ricciotti Greatti](/wiki/Ricciotti_Greatti "Ricciotti Greatti"), and forward [Gigi Riva](/wiki/Gigi_Riva "Gigi Riva"). A poor first half of the season, however, saw Cagliari in last place with nine points at the halfway mark. An astonishing second half of the season saw Cagliari defeat the likes of [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_F.C. "Juventus F.C.") and [Milan](/wiki/A.C._Milan "A.C. Milan") and finish in seventh place with 34 points. Two seasons later, Riva finished as Serie A's top scorer for the first time while Cagliari finished with the league's best defensive record.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Gigi_Riva_-_US_Cagliari_%281970%29_-_CROPPED.jpg/250px-Gigi_Riva_-_US_Cagliari_%281970%29_-_CROPPED.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gigi_Riva_-_US_Cagliari_(1970)_-_CROPPED.jpg)

Forward [Gigi Riva](/wiki/Gigi_Riva "Gigi Riva") led Cagliari to their first [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") title in 1969–70.

During the summer of 1967, Cagliari played a season in North America as part of a fledgling league called the [United Soccer Association](/wiki/United_Soccer_Association "United Soccer Association"). This league included teams from Europe and South America set to play in American and Canadian cities, with each club bearing a local name. Cagliari played as the [Chicago Mustangs](/wiki/Chicago_Mustangs_(1967%E2%80%9368) "Chicago Mustangs (1967–68)"), and finished joint second in the league's Western Division with 13 points, two behind the division champion and eventual league champion [Los Angeles Wolves](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Wolves "Los Angeles Wolves"). The league's leading scorer was Chicago/Cagliari's [Roberto Boninsegna](/wiki/Roberto_Boninsegna "Roberto Boninsegna"), who scored ten goals while playing in 9 of the team's 12 games.

Cagliari first emerged as serious Serie A title contenders in [1968–69](/wiki/1968%E2%80%9369_Serie_A "1968–69 Serie A") with a three-horse race involving them, [Fiorentina](/wiki/ACF_Fiorentina "ACF Fiorentina") and Milan. Fiorentina would win the league, but the following season would bring ultimate glory. With [Angelo Domenghini](/wiki/Angelo_Domenghini "Angelo Domenghini") joining the side, Cagliari would win the title in 1970 with only two games lost, 11 goals conceded (the fewest in any major European league to date) and Riva as league top scorer once more. Players like Albertosi, Niccolai, Boninsegna, Gori, Cera, Domenghini and Riva played in Italy's [1970 World Cup](/wiki/1970_FIFA_World_Cup "1970 FIFA World Cup") final team.

The 1970s would see a gradual decline (though were title contenders two years after their one and only *Scudetto* win). Cagliari were finally relegated in 1976, with Riva's career having effectively ended during that season.

### Up and down again: 1976–87

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cagliari_Calcio&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Up and down again: 1976–87")]

After relegation, Cagliari lost a play-off for promotion the following season and would return to Serie A in 1979. Players like [Franco Selvaggi](/wiki/Franco_Selvaggi "Franco Selvaggi"), [Mario Brugnera](/w/index.php?title=Mario_Brugnera&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mario Brugnera (page does not exist)") (a survivor of the 1970 team) and [Alberto Marchetti](/wiki/Alberto_Marchetti "Alberto Marchetti") ensured a respectable four-year stay in the top flight before a second relegation in 1983. The 1980s would then prove to be a darker time compared to the previous two decades with relegation to Serie C1 in 1987.

### There and back: 1987–2000

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cagliari_Calcio&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: There and back: 1987–2000")]

Cagliari spent two seasons in Serie C1. In the first one it barely avoided relegation in Serie C2. In 1988, [Claudio Ranieri](/wiki/Claudio_Ranieri "Claudio Ranieri") was appointed coach, and led the team to two successive promotions, to Serie B in 1989 and to Serie A in 1990. The first two seasons back in Serie A saw Cagliari fight relegation, with safety being achieved by excellent second half runs. But the [1992–93 season](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_Serie_A "1992–93 Serie A") would see Cagliari fight for a European place and succeed under the management of [Carlo Mazzone](/wiki/Carlo_Mazzone "Carlo Mazzone"). The following season saw a best-ever run to the [semi-finals](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_UEFA_Cup#Semi-finals "1993–94 UEFA Cup") of the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League"), taking out [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus "Juventus") in the quarter-finals before being eliminated 5–3 on aggregate by compatriots [Internazionale](/wiki/Internazionale "Internazionale"), having won the first leg 3–2 at home.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The next few years would see Cagliari return to mid-table anonymity, before a struggle in [1996–97](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_Serie_A "1996–97 Serie A") saw Cagliari relegated after losing a play-off to [Piacenza](/wiki/Piacenza_Calcio_1919 "Piacenza Calcio 1919"). Once more they bounced back after just one year, but their next stay in Serie A lasted just two seasons.

### Once and again: 2000 onwards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cagliari_Calcio&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Once and again: 2000 onwards")]

Cagliari spent the next four seasons in Serie B, until in [2003–04](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_Serie_B "2003–04 Serie B") with Sardinian-born veteran striker [Gianfranco Zola](/wiki/Gianfranco_Zola "Gianfranco Zola"), the team won promotion.[[2]](#cite_note-2) In [2005–06](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Serie_A "2005–06 Serie A"), the first season without Zola, the team changed their manager three times before [Nedo Sonetti](/wiki/Nedo_Sonetti "Nedo Sonetti"), appointed in November, was able to save the team from relegation, especially thanks to the excellent goal contribution from Honduran striker [David Suazo](/wiki/David_Suazo "David Suazo").

Apart from finishing 9th in [2008–09 season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Serie_A "2008–09 Serie A"), Cagliari regularly finished in the bottom half of Serie A under a sequence of managers, before being relegated in [2014–15](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Serie_A "2014–15 Serie A").[[3]](#cite_note-3) They gained promotion back the following season as champions of Serie B.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In 2014, the company passed, after 22 years of [Massimo Cellino](/wiki/Massimo_Cellino "Massimo Cellino")'s presidency, into the hands of Tommaso Giulini, president and owner of [Fluorsid](/wiki/Fluorsid "Fluorsid"), a multinational in the chemical sector. Relegation took place in the first season, but the team won the [Serie B championship](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Serie_B "2015–16 Serie B") in 2016, returning permanently to the top division, albeit always finishing in the second half of the table. Cagliari was relegated at the end of the [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Cagliari_Calcio_season "2021–22 Cagliari Calcio season"). They reappointed Claudio Ranieri halfway through the [2022–23 season](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_Cagliari_Calcio_season "2022–23 Cagliari Calcio season") with the club in fourteenth place. Under Ranieri's management, Cagliari won the [2022–23 Serie B playoffs](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_Serie_B "2022–23 Serie B").

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cagliari_Calcio&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Stadium")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Stadio_Sant%27Elia_-Cagliari_-Italy-23Oct2008_crop.jpg/250px-Stadio_Sant%27Elia_-Cagliari_-Italy-23Oct2008_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stadio_Sant%27Elia_-Cagliari_-Italy-23Oct2008_crop.jpg)

Cagliari played at the [Stadio Sant'Elia](/wiki/Stadio_Sant%27Elia "Stadio Sant'Elia") from 1970 to 2017.

Cagliari moved from the [Stadio Amsicora](/wiki/Stadio_Amsicora "Stadio Amsicora") to the [Stadio Sant'Elia](/wiki/Stadio_Sant%27Elia "Stadio Sant'Elia") in 1970, after winning their only league title. It was renovated for Italy's hosting of the [1990 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup") where it hosted all of [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team")'s group games, ostensibly to confine the team's notorious hooligans to an island.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Disputes with the city council over renovation of the publicly owned stadium meant that Cagliari played their final home games of 2011–12 at the [Stadio Nereo Rocco](/wiki/Stadio_Nereo_Rocco "Stadio Nereo Rocco") in [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste") on the Italian mainland.[[6]](#cite_note-6) For most of the following season, the club played at the [Stadio Is Arenas](/wiki/Stadio_Is_Arenas "Stadio Is Arenas") in the neighbouring municipality of [Quartu Sant'Elena](/wiki/Quartu_Sant%27Elena "Quartu Sant'Elena"). It was deemed unsafe by the league, forcing them to play behind closed doors before leaving the ground in April 2013.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The Sant'Elia was demolished for a new stadium in 2017, and the club moved to the temporary [Unipol Domus](/wiki/Unipol_Domus "Unipol Domus") next to it.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Colours, badge and nicknames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cagliari_Calcio&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Colours, badge and nicknames")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Flag_of_Sardinia%2C_Italy.svg/250px-Flag_of_Sardinia%2C_Italy.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Sardinia,_Italy.svg)

Cagliari's badge incorporates the [flag of Sardinia](/wiki/Flag_of_Sardinia "Flag of Sardinia").

The official red and blue colours of Cagliari mirror those featured on the *stemma* of [Cagliari](/wiki/Cagliari "Cagliari").[[9]](#cite_note-colours-9) The red parts of the stemma are a reference to the [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") of the [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy"), a family which was previously the monarchy of Italy and more relevantly to Cagliari in particular, the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia "Kingdom of Sardinia").[[9]](#cite_note-colours-9) The blue part of the *stemma* features the sky and the sea, also a [castle](/wiki/Castle "Castle"); this is because the old historic centre of Cagliari is walled and called the *Castello*.[[9]](#cite_note-colours-9) Due to the use of these colours on their shirt in halves, the club is commonly nicknamed *rossoblu*.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Cagliari have had several different logo designs during their history, all of which feature the [flag of Sardinia](/wiki/Flag_of_Sardinia "Flag of Sardinia").[[11]](#cite_note-badge-11) Usually the badge also features the club colours; if there is a change, the main difference has been the colour of the border or the shape.[[11]](#cite_note-badge-11) Since June 2015, the badge features an "Old French"-shaped [escutcheon](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)") with red and blue halves, with the club's name written in white just above the flag of Sardinia. The Moors' heads have, for the first time, been turned to the right as of 2015 so as to match the Sardinian flag after it was updated in 1992.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Because Cagliari are the main club from the island of Sardinia, they are nicknamed the "*Isolani*" ("Islanders").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Supporters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cagliari_Calcio&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Supporters")]

In the 1970s, the ultras movement developed, as in Italy. The first group was founded in 1977 with the name of Brigate Rossoblù, which was joined by Fossa Ultrà, with the latter progressing in the early 1980s to follow the team also on the "Continent" and to meet with other opposing fans. However, the phenomenon also waned due to the team's downward trajectory in that decade after the glories of the Scudetto. However, the Commando Ultrà Young Supporters was born and later merged with the Ultrà Cagliari. In February 1987 some of the members of these groups gave rise to the Sconvolts who were joined by an already existing group, the Eagles. Two years later, in 1989, Furiosi were also born. These two groups monopolized the hottest support throughout the 1990s and early 2000s, never bonding as happened with the previous groups and indeed leading to an internal struggle which led to the dissolution of the Furiosi in 2004 and led the Sconvolts group to still be the only ultras fringe remaining in the Curva Nord of the various stadiums that have taken place in recent years.

## Friendships and Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cagliari_Calcio&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Friendships and Rivalries")]

The Sconvolts the historical group of the Cagliari maintain relationships of friendship and respect with [Olbia](/wiki/Olbia_Calcio "Olbia Calcio"), [Atalanta](/wiki/Atalanta_Calcio "Atalanta Calcio"), [Parma](/wiki/Parma_Calcio "Parma Calcio"), [U.S. Lecce](/wiki/U.S._Lecce "U.S. Lecce").

Friendships from the past that no longer exist: [Foggia](/wiki/Foggia_Calcio "Foggia Calcio"), [Sampdoria](/wiki/Unione_Calcio_Sampdoria "Unione Calcio Sampdoria"), [Inter FC](/wiki/Internazionale_Football_Club "Internazionale Football Club")

The main rivalries are with the [Torres](/wiki/SEF_Torres_1903 "SEF Torres 1903") with which it is the [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia") Derby and [Napoli](/wiki/Societ%C3%A0_Sportiva_Calcio_Napoli "Società Sportiva Calcio Napoli").
Other rivalries: [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo_Football_Club "Palermo Football Club"), [S.S.C. Bari](/wiki/S.S.C._Bari "S.S.C. Bari"), [Hellas Verona](/wiki/Hellas_Verona_Football_Club "Hellas Verona Football Club"), [A.C.Milan](/wiki/Associazione_Calcio_Milan "Associazione Calcio Milan"), [Juventus FC](/wiki/Juventus_Football_Club "Juventus Football Club"), Inter FC, [Catania](/wiki/Calcio_Catania "Calcio Catania"), [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa_Cricket_and_Football_Club "Genoa Cricket and Football Club"), [Brescia](/wiki/Brescia_Calcio "Brescia Calcio"), [Venezia FC](/wiki/Venezia_Football_Club "Venezia Football Club"), [Lazio](/wiki/SS_Lazio "SS Lazio"), [ACF Fiorentina](/wiki/ACF_Fiorentina "ACF Fiorentina"), [Salernitana 1919](/wiki/Salernitana_1919 "Salernitana 1919"), [Ancona](/wiki/Associazione_Calcio_Ancona "Associazione Calcio Ancona"), [Pogoń Szczecin](/wiki/Pogo%C5%84_Szczecin "Pogoń Szczecin").
